1: hēdonōn hymōn tōn strateuomenōn en tois melesin hymōn — from your pleasures (hēdonōn) that war (strateuomenōn, making war, present participle) among (en) your members. The military metaphor is vivid: the passions are soldiers conducting a campaign in the body — internal warfare producing external conflicts. Hēdonē (pleasure/desire) is used negatively throughout Greek ethical writing for undisciplined desire; it is the root of 'hedonism.' The question pothen (from where?) seeks the origin of the community's conflicts and locates it not in external circumstances but in the inner life of desire.
2: epithymeite kai ouk echete... phoneuete kai zēloute kai ou dynasthe epitychein — the sequence desire → lack → murder, covet → cannot obtain → conflict represents an escalating pattern. The use of phoneuete (murder) in a letter to a religious community is unexpected and has generated debate: is it literal, hyperbolic, or metaphorical (hatred as a form of murder, cf. 1 John 3:15)? The Greek machathe kai polemeite (you fight and war) distinguishes personal quarrels (machesthai) from larger organized conflict (polemeite). The diagnosis: desire without prayer produces violence rather than satisfaction.
3: katanadapanenēn en tais hēdonais hymōn — that you may spend it on your pleasures (dapanēsēte en tais hēdonais hymōn). The verb dapanaō (spend, use up wastefully) is the same root as the prodigal son's 'squandering' in Luke 15:14 (Greek). Asking wrongly (kakōs) is specifically asking for the purpose of fueling one's desires. James locates the failure not in prayer itself but in its motivation: prayer addressed to God for self-serving pleasures is prayer that treats God as a means to personal gratification rather than as the source and goal of all true blessing.
4: moichalides — adulterous people (moichalides, literally 'adulteresses' — the feminine form, used metaphorically for the community's spiritual infidelity). The OT prophets (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel) consistently used marriage and adultery as the metaphor for the covenant relationship between YHWH and Israel: idolatry and worldliness are adultery against the divine husband. James picks up this prophetic tradition: friendship with the world (philia tou kosmou) is hostility toward God (echthra tou theou). The world here is not creation but the world-system organized around the desires and values that oppose God.
5: pros phthonon epipothei to pneuma ho katōkisen en hēmin — this is the most difficult verse in James. The Greek may be translated: (a) 'The spirit he caused to dwell in us yearns jealously' (God's jealous Spirit longing for our full devotion) or (b) 'The spirit he made to dwell in us craves enviously' (the human spirit tends toward envy). The text is introduced as a scriptural citation (legei hē graphē) but no OT text matches it precisely. Option (a) draws on the OT's pervasive 'jealous God' tradition (Exod 20:5; 34:14; Zech 8:2), where YHWH's jealousy is his fierce covenantal commitment to having his people's full devotion. Most commentators prefer (a): God's Spirit yearns jealously for exclusive devotion from those in whom he dwells.
6: meizona de didōsin charin — he gives all the more/greater grace (meizona de didōsin charin). The adversative de (but) marks the contrast with the jealousy of v5: God's jealousy is not punitive hoarding but the passion of one who gives greater grace precisely because he wants full devotion. The citation from Prov 3:34 LXX follows: ho theos hyperēphanois antitassetai, tapeinois de didōsin charin (God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble). The 'proud' (hyperēphanoi — those who display themselves above others) and 'humble' (tapeinoi) recall the rich/poor and the high/low of ch.2. Grace is the counter to the self-assertion that destroys community.
7: hypotassō / anthistēmi — submit (hypotassō, arrange yourself under) and resist (anthistēmi, stand against). These are military-administrative verbs: hypotassō is used for troops arranging themselves under a commander; anthistēmi is used for troops standing firm against an advance. The two commands structure the believer's posture in the spiritual warfare described in v1: submission to God (the opposite of the friendship-with-the-world in v4) and resistance to the devil. The promise that the devil will flee (pheuxetai, future tense) follows the pattern of Matt 4:11 (the devil departing after Jesus' resistance in the wilderness) — resistance to temptation is not futile but effective.
8: eggisate tō theō kai eggiei hymin — draw near to God and he will draw near to you (eggisate... eggiei). The mutual-approach structure is found in the OT's approach-to-YHWH theology: the covenant relationship is not static but dynamic, and God responds to human turning toward him (Zech 1:3; Mal 3:7: 'return to me, and I will return to you'). The following imperatives — cleanse your hands (katharisate cheiras, cultic hand-washing language) and purify your hearts (hagnisate kardias, the inner cleansing) — apply the outer/inner distinction that the prophets made: Isa 1:15-17 demanded both clean hands and a just heart. Dipsychoi (double-minded, v8) reappears from 1:8: the restoration path for the dipsychos is drawing near to God with a cleansed, undivided heart.
9: talaiporēsate kai penthēsate kai klausate — be wretched and mourn and weep (talaiporēsate kai penthēsate kai klausate). The three imperatives describe genuine sorrow over sin — not performative grief but the visceral recognition of the seriousness of the divided-heart condition described in v4-8. Gelōs (laughter) and chara (joy) are to become mourning (penthos) and grief (katēpheia, dejection — the downcast face of genuine remorse). This is not a permanent posture (the promise of v10 is exaltation) but the necessary movement through genuine repentance rather than a cheap turning that bypasses the weight of what has been lost.
10: tapeinōthēte enōpion kyriou kai hypsōsei hymas — humble yourselves before the Lord and he will exalt you (hypsōsei, future active — not a possibility but a promise). The passive-voiced humbling (tapeinōthēte, aorist passive imperative) is significant: the person is to allow themselves to be humbled, to place themselves in the lowly position before God. The exaltation is entirely God's act. The same paradox as 1:9-10, the Beatitudes (Matt 5:3), and the Magnificat (Luke 1:52): the lowering of the proud and the lifting of the humble. This promise provides the motive for the painful process of vv8-9: the outcome of drawing near in genuine repentance is not permanent depression but divine exaltation.
11: katalaleō / krinōn... ton nomon — to speak evil of (katalaleō, to speak down against) and to judge a brother is to speak evil of and judge the law. The logic: the community lives under the nomos tēs eleutherias (law of liberty) as the governing principle; when you appoint yourself as judge of a brother, you are implicitly positioning yourself above the law (as one who administers it) rather than under it (as one who is governed by it). Ou poiētēs nomou alla kritēs (not a doer of the law but a judge of it) — the contrast between doer (poiētēs) and judge (kritēs) picks up the doer/hearer contrast of 1:22-25.
12: heis estin ho nomothetēs kai kritēs — there is one Lawgiver and Judge (heis estin ho nomothetēs kai kritēs). The absolute singularity of the Lawgiver-Judge repeats the Shema's monotheistic logic of 2:19 but applies it to moral governance: just as there is one God, there is one judge, and presuming to judge one's brother is claiming a divine prerogative. Ho dynamenos sōsai kai apolesai (the one able to save and to destroy) — the dual ability echoes Deut 32:39 ('I put to death and I bring to life, I have wounded and I will heal') and Matt 10:28 (the one who can destroy both soul and body in Gehenna). Only this God has the standing to judge.
13: age nyn — come now (age nyn) — the same transitional phrase as 5:1. The form is a call to attention, used in Greek rhetoric to introduce a challenging charge. The wealthy merchants are addressed indirectly (not named or addressed directly): their planning speech is quoted ('today or tomorrow we will go to this city...') and then evaluated. The confidence of their commercial planning — the city, the year, the profit — assumes a stability of future they do not possess.
14: atmis gar estin hē pros oligon phainomenē, epeita kai aphanizomenē — a mist (atmis) that appears for a little while and then disappears. The word atmis (vapor/mist/steam) is used in LXX Ps 102:3 (NIV 103:3: 'like smoke, my days vanish') and Joel 2:30 (vapor/smoke) for the transience of created existence. The dual participle structure (phainomenē, appearing; aphanizomenē, disappearing — sharing the same stem phain, light/appear) captures the verb visually: the mist appears with brief visibility and then vanishes into invisibility.
15: ean ho kyrios thelēsē — if the Lord wills (ean ho kyrios thelēsē). This is the deo volente (God willing) formula, common in both Jewish and early Christian practice. Paul uses it repeatedly (1 Cor 4:19; 16:7; Acts 18:21). The conditional form (ean, if) is not doubt about God's existence but acknowledgment of the contingency of all human planning on the divine will. The contrast with the merchant's bold declarations ('we will go... we will spend... we will make a profit') is sharp: the appropriate grammar for human future plans is subjunctive-conditional, not indicative-declarative.
16: kauchāsthe en tais alazoniais hymōn — you boast (kauchāsthe) in your arrogance (alazoniais, plural of alazonia — braggart/self-assertion). Alazonia is used in 1 John 2:16 ('the boastful pride of life') as one of the characteristics of the world-system. The boaster (alazon) in Greek characterization was the person who claimed more than they possessed or more than was true. James identifies commercial planning that ignores God's sovereignty as this kind of hubris: not just imprudent but actively arrogant, claiming a sovereignty over time and outcome that belongs only to God.
17: eidoti kalon poiein kai mē poionti — to the one who knows (eidoti) the good to do and does not do it (mē poionti), it is sin. The knowledge-action gap is the Jamesian form of the doer/hearer distinction from 1:22-25. The verse functions as a summary of the chapter: the merchants know the right posture ('if the Lord wills'), the people in vv7-10 know the right response to God (draw near, submit, humble), and yet choosing not to do these known goods is itself a form of sin — not merely ignorance but willful non-action in the face of knowledge.
